combined_corpus.title: Validity of Off-the-Shelf Automatic Speech Recognition for Assessing Speech Intelligibility and Speech Severity in Speakers With Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis.
combined_corpus.journal: Journal of speech, language, and hearing research : JSLHR
combined_corpus.abstract: There is increasing interest in using automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems to evaluate impairment severity or speech intelligibility in speakers with dysarthria. We assessed the clinical validity of one currently available off-the-shelf (OTS) ASR system (i.e., a Google Cloud ASR API) for indexing sentence-level speech intelligibility and impairment severity in individuals with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), and we provided guidance for potential users of such systems in research and clinic. Using speech samples collected from 52 individuals with ALS and 20 healthy control speakers, we compared word recognition rate (WRR) from the commercially available Google Cloud ASR API (Machine WRR) to clinician-provided judgments of impairment severity, as well as sentence intelligibility (Human WRR). We assessed the internal reliability of Machine and Human WRR by comparing the standard deviation of WRR across sentences to the minimally detectable change (MDC), a clinical benchmark that indicates whether results are within measurement error. We also evaluated Machine and Human WRR diagnostic accuracy for classifying speakers into clinically established categories. Human WRR achieved better accuracy than Machine WRR when indexing speech severity, and, although related, Human and Machine WRR were not strongly correlated. When the speech signal was mixed with noise (noise-augmented ASR) to reduce a ceiling effect, Machine WRR performance improved. Internal reliability metrics were worse for Machine than Human WRR, particularly for typical and mildly impaired severity groups, although sentence length significantly impacted both Machine and Human WRRs. Results indicated that the OTS ASR system was inadequate for early detection of speech impairment and grading overall speech severity. While Machine and Human WRR were correlated, ASR should not be used as a one-to-one proxy for transcription speech intelligibility or clinician severity ratings. Overall, findings suggested that the tested OTS ASR system, Google Cloud ASR, has limited utility for grading clinical speech impairment in speakers with ALS.

combined_corpus.title: The use of speech recognition technology by people living with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis: a scoping review.
combined_corpus.abstract: More than 80% of people living with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (plwALS) develop difficulties with their speech, affecting communication, self-identity and quality of life. Automatic speech recognition technology (ASR) is becoming a common way to interact with a broad range of devices, to find information and control the environment.ASR can be problematic for people with acquired neurogenic motor speech difficulties (dysarthria). Given that the field is rapidly developing, a scoping review is warranted. This study undertakes a scoping review on the use of ASR technology by plwALS and identifies research gaps in the existing literature. Electronic databases and relevant grey literature were searched from 1990 to 2020. Eleven research papers and articles were identified that included participants living with ALS using ASR technology. Relevant data were extracted from the included sources, and a narrative summary of the findings presented.Outcomes and Results: Eleven publications used recordings of plwALS to assess word recognition rate (WRR) word error rate (WER) or phoneme error rate (PER) and appropriacy of responses by ASR devices. All were found to be linked to severity of dysarthria and the ASR technology used. One article examined how speech modification may improve ASR accuracy. The final article completed thematic analysis of Amazon.com reviews for the Amazon Echo and plwALS were reported to use ASR devices to control the environment and summon assistance. There are gaps in the evidence base: understanding expectations of plwALS and how they use ASR technology; how WER/PER/WRR relates to usability; how ASR use changes as ALS progresses.Implications for rehabilitationDevices that people can interact with using speech are becoming ubiquitous. As movement and mobility are likely to be affected by ALS and progress over time, speech interaction could be very helpful for accessing information and environmental control.However, many people living with ALS (plwALS) also have impaired speech (dysarthria) and experience trouble using voice interaction technology because it may not understand them.Although advances in automated speech recognition (ASR) technology promise better understanding of dysarthric speech, future research needs to investigate how plwALS use ASR, how accurate it needs to be to be functionally useful, and how useful it may be over time as the disease progresses.

combined_corpus.title: OBJECTIVE ASSESSMENT OF VOCAL TREMOR.
combined_corpus.journal: Proceedings of the ... IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing. ICASSP (Conference)
combined_corpus.abstract: Detecting early signs of neurodegeneration is vital for planning treatments for neurological diseases. Speech plays an important role in this context because it has been shown to be a promising early indicator of neurological decline, and because it can be acquired remotely without the need for specialized hardware. Typically, symptoms are characterized by clinicians using subjective and discrete scales. The poor resolution and subjectivity of these scales can make the earliest speech changes hard to detect. In this paper, we propose an algorithm for the objective assessment of vocal tremor, a phenomenon associated with many neurological disorders. The algorithm extracts and aggregates a feature set from the average spectra of the energy and fundamental frequency profiles of a sustained phonation. We show that the resultant low-dimensional feature set reliably classifies healthy controls and patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis perceptually rated for tremor by speech language pathologists.

combined_corpus.title: Evaluation of Digital Technologies for Home-Based Assessment in People With Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis.
combined_corpus.journal: Annals of clinical and translational neurology
combined_corpus.abstract: Digital technologies hold promise for transforming healthcare by enhancing personalized treatments and offer valuable opportunities to improve patient care. Here, we evaluated several novel, self-administered, home-based, digital endpoints for their association with corresponding conventional standard clinical measures (primary) in people living with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). This was a longitudinal study in people with ALS who were followed up to 9 months. A total of 33 participants were enrolled in the study. At each of six visits, participants were evaluated with a battery of conventional standard measurements to determine ALS disease progression and quality of life. Between visits, participants performed weekly home-based self-assessments with digital health technologies (DHT) and self-administered ALSFRS-R. Cross-sectional analysis of DHTs anchored to ALSFRS-R and longitudinal analyses were performed and compared to standard clinical measures. Of the 33 participants, 20 completed the study, and 13 discontinued before completing the planned 9-month follow-up mainly due to disease progression. The distribution of various digital metrics in home-based assessments corresponded well with the sub-scores of ALSFRS-R in the cross-sectional analyses, with the strongest construct validity for digital speaking rate. In the longitudinal analysis, a weak but significant trend in most metrics was observed, with the strongest trend in the duration of the Timed Up and Go (high variability between participants). The findings from this study provide insights into the potential of digital endpoints to evaluate people living with ALS with the goal of reducing the burden of study participation and improving the efficiency of ALS clinical trials.

At each of six visits, participants were evaluated with a battery of conventional standard measurements to determine ALS disease progression and quality of life. Between visits, participants performed weekly home-based self-assessments with digital health technologies (DHT) and self-administered ALSFRS-R. Cross-sectional analysis of DHTs anchored to ALSFRS-R and longitudinal analyses were performed and compared to standard clinical measures. Of the 33 participants, 20 completed the study, and 13 discontinued before completing the planned 9-month follow-up mainly due to disease progression. The distribution of various digital metrics in home-based assessments corresponded well with the sub-scores of ALSFRS-R in the cross-sectional analyses, with the strongest construct validity for digital speaking rate. In the longitudinal analysis, a weak but significant trend in most metrics was observed, with the strongest trend in the duration of the Timed Up and Go (high variability between participants). The findings from this study provide insights into the potential of digital endpoints to evaluate people living with ALS with the goal of reducing the burden of study participation and improving the efficiency of ALS clinical trials.

combined_corpus.title: A Preliminary Investigation of Acoustic Features for Remote Monitoring of Respiration in ALS.
combined_corpus.abstract: There is a substantial need to establish reliable approaches for low-burden at-home monitoring of respiratory function for people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (PALS). This preliminary study assessed the potential of acoustic features extracted from a smartphone passage reading task to serve as clinically meaningful outcome measures reflecting instrumental and self-reported respiratory function measures. Thirty-six PALS completed an in-clinic slow vital capacity (SVC) task, followed by at-home completion of surveys and audio recording of a reading passage using a smartphone application. Speaking rate and pause features were extracted offline. Correlation analysis evaluated the relationship between the acoustic features and both instrumental (SVC) and self-reported (respiratory subscale of the self-entry version of the ALS Functional Rating Scale-Revised; ALSFRS-RSE) measures of respiratory function. Receiver operator characteristic (ROC) with area under the curve (AUC) analysis evaluated the utility of acoustic features for classifying participants with and without respiratory involvement. SVC and respiratory self-ratings were significantly correlated with pause, but not rate, measures. Percent pause time was the most strongly correlated acoustic feature with both SVC (r = -0.62) and ALSFRS-RSE respiratory subscale ratings (r = -0.43). ROC analysis revealed that percent pause time classified participants presenting with respiratory involvement based on instrumentation (SVC < 70% predicted [AUC = 0.70]; SVC < 50% predicted [AUC = 0.88]) and self-ratings when using the respiratory ALSFRS-RSE score cut-off of < 11 (AUC = 0.78), but not < 12 (AUC = 0.61). Percent pause time, extracted from a smartphone-recorded passage reading, offers a promising index for remote assessment and monitoring of respiratory function in PALS.

combined_corpus.title: Estimation of forced vital capacity using speech acoustics in patients with ALS.
combined_corpus.abstract: In this study, we present and provide validation data for a tool that predicts forced vital capacity (FVC) from speech acoustics collected remotely via a mobile app without the need for any additional equipment (e.g. a spirometer). We trained a machine learning model on a sample of healthy participants and participants with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) to learn a mapping from speech acoustics to FVC and used this model to predict FVC values in a new sample from a different study of participants with ALS. We further evaluated the cross-sectional accuracy of the model and its sensitivity to within-subject change in FVC. We found that the predicted and observed FVC values in the test sample had a correlation coefficient of .80 and mean absolute error between .54 L and .58 L (18.5% to 19.5%). In addition, we found that the model was able to detect longitudinal decline in FVC in the test sample, although to a lesser extent than the observed FVC values measured using a spirometer, and was highly repeatable (ICC = 0.92-0.94), although to a lesser extent than the actual FVC (ICC = .97). These results suggest that sustained phonation may be a useful surrogate for VC in both research and clinical environments.

combined_corpus.title: Speech-based digital endpoints track ALS progression and align with standard clinical outcomes: evidence from the VRG50635 trial.
combined_corpus.abstract: We report on the utility of speech-based digital endpoints measured during a Phase 1b study of VRG50635 in Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). Fifty-four participants with ALS were enrolled and participated in an 8-week pretreatment run-in, followed by three 8-week dosing periods and an 8-week follow-up. They completed a speech assessment every two weeks in the clinic or at home. We observed moderate to high correlations between digital measures of speech timing and articulatory motor function, and the ALS Functional Rating Scale-Revised, slow vital capacity and plasma neurofilament light chain. Furthermore, speech measures can show functional decline before the ALSFRS-R does, while also capturing differences between participants with bulbar symptoms and those without. The results support the feasibility and utility of digital speech endpoints to study disease impact in ALS clinical trials.

combined_corpus.title: Multimodal speech biomarkers for remote monitoring of ALS disease progression.
combined_corpus.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease that severely impacts affected persons' speech and motor functions, yet early detection and tracking of disease progression remain challenging. The current gold standard for monitoring ALS progression, the ALS functional rating scale - revised (ALSFRS-R), is based on subjective ratings of symptom severity, and may not capture subtle but clinically meaningful changes due to a lack of granularity. Multimodal speech measures which can be automatically collected from patients in a remote fashion allow us to bridge this gap because they are continuous-valued and therefore, potentially more granular at capturing disease progression. Here we investigate the responsiveness and sensitivity of multimodal speech measures in persons with ALS (pALS) collected via a remote patient monitoring platform in an effort to quantify how long it takes to detect a clinically-meaningful change associated with disease progression. We recorded audio and video from 278 participants and automatically extracted multimodal speech biomarkers (acoustic, orofacial, linguistic) from the data. We find that the timing alignment of pALS speech relative to a canonical elicitation of the same prompt and the number of words used to describe a picture are the most responsive measures at detecting such change in both pALS with bulbar (n = 36) and non-bulbar onset (n = 107). Interestingly, the responsiveness of these measures is stable even at small sample sizes. We further found that certain speech measures are sensitive enough to track bulbar decline even when there is no patient-reported clinical change, i.e. the ALSFRS-R speech score remains unchanged at 3 out of a total possible score of 4. The findings of this study have the potential to facilitate improved, accelerated and cost-effective clinical trials and care.

combined_corpus.title: Responsiveness, Sensitivity and Clinical Utility of Timing-Related Speech Biomarkers for Remote Monitoring of ALS Disease Progression.
combined_corpus.abstract: In this study, we describe the responsiveness of timing-related measures extracted from read speech in persons with ALS (pALS) collected via a remote patient monitoring platform in an effort to quantify how long it takes to detect a clinically-meaningful change associated with disease progression. We found that the timing alignment of pALS speech relative to a canonical elicitation of the same prompt is the most responsive measure, of the ones considered in this study, at detecting such change in both pALS with bulbar (n = 35) and non-bulbar onset (n = 94). We further evaluated the sensitivity of speech metrics in tracking disease progression in pALS while their ALSFRS-R speech score remained unchanged at 3 out of a total possible score of 4. We observed that timing-related speech metrics showed significant longitudinal changes even after accounting for learning effects. The findings of this study have the potential to inform disease prognosis and functional outcomes of clinical trials.

combined_corpus.title: A machine-learning based objective measure for ALS disease severity.
combined_corpus.abstract: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) disease severity is usually measured using the subjective, questionnaire-based revised ALS Functional Rating Scale (ALSFRS-R). Objective measures of disease severity would be powerful tools for evaluating real-world drug effectiveness, efficacy in clinical trials, and for identifying participants for cohort studies. We developed a machine learning (ML) based objective measure for ALS disease severity based on voice samples and accelerometer measurements from a four-year longitudinal dataset. 584 people living with ALS consented and carried out prescribed speaking and limb-based tasks. 542 participants contributed 5814 voice recordings, and 350 contributed 13,009 accelerometer samples, while simultaneously measuring ALSFRS-R scores. Using these data, we trained ML models to predict bulbar-related and limb-related ALSFRS-R scores. On the test set (n = 109 participants) the voice models achieved a multiclass AUC of 0.86 (95% CI, 0.85-0.88) on speech ALSFRS-R prediction, whereas the accelerometer models achieved a median multiclass AUC of 0.73 on 6 limb-related functions. The correlations across functions observed in self-reported ALSFRS-R scores were preserved in ML-derived scores. We used these models and self-reported ALSFRS-R scores to evaluate the real-world effects of edaravone, a drug approved for use in ALS. In the cohort of 54 test participants who received edaravone as part of their usual care, the ML-derived scores were consistent with the self-reported ALSFRS-R scores. At the individual level, the continuous ML-derived score can capture gradual changes that are absent in the integer ALSFRS-R scores. This demonstrates the value of these tools for assessing disease severity and, potentially, drug effects.

On the test set (n = 109 participants) the voice models achieved a multiclass AUC of 0.86 (95% CI, 0.85-0.88) on speech ALSFRS-R prediction, whereas the accelerometer models achieved a median multiclass AUC of 0.73 on 6 limb-related functions. The correlations across functions observed in self-reported ALSFRS-R scores were preserved in ML-derived scores. We used these models and self-reported ALSFRS-R scores to evaluate the real-world effects of edaravone, a drug approved for use in ALS. In the cohort of 54 test participants who received edaravone as part of their usual care, the ML-derived scores were consistent with the self-reported ALSFRS-R scores. At the individual level, the continuous ML-derived score can capture gradual changes that are absent in the integer ALSFRS-R scores. This demonstrates the value of these tools for assessing disease severity and, potentially, drug effects.

combined_corpus.title: Early detection and tracking of bulbar changes in ALS via frequent and remote speech analysis.
combined_corpus.abstract: Bulbar deterioration in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a devastating characteristic that impairs patients' ability to communicate, and is linked to shorter survival. The existing clinical instruments for assessing bulbar function lack sensitivity to early changes. In this paper, using a cohort of N = 65 ALS patients who provided regular speech samples for 3-9 months, we demonstrated that it is possible to remotely detect early speech changes and track speech progression in ALS via automated algorithmic assessment of speech collected digitally.

combined_corpus.title: The use of biotelemetry to explore disease progression markers in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.
combined_corpus.abstract: To explore novel, real-world biotelemetry disease progression markers in patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and to compare with clinical gold-standard measures. Methods: This was an exploratory, non-controlled, non-drug 2-phase study comprising a variable length Pilot Phase (n = 5) and a 48-week Core study Phase (n = 25; NCT02447952). Patients with mild or moderate ALS wore biotelemetry sensors for ∼3 days/month at home, measuring physical activity, heart rate variability (HRV), and speech over 48 weeks. These measures were assessed longitudinally in relation to ALS Functional Rating Scale-Revised (ALSFRS-R) score and forced vital capacity (FVC); assessed by telephone [monthly] and clinic visits [every 12 weeks]). Results: Pilot Phase data supported progression into the Core Phase, where a decline in physical activity from baseline followed ALS progression as measured by ALSFRS-R and FVC. Four endpoints showed moderate or strong between-patient correlations with ALSFRS-R total and gross motor domain scores (defined as a correlation coefficient of ≥0.5 or >0.7, respectively): average daytime active; percentage of daytime active; total daytime activity score; total 24-hour activity score. Moderate correlations were observed between speech endpoints and ALSFRS-R bulbar domain scores; HRV data quality was insufficient for reliable assessment. The sensor was generally well tolerated; 6/25 patients reported mostly mild or moderate intensity skin and subcutaneous tissue disorder adverse events. Conclusions: Biotelemetry measures of physical activity in this Pilot Study tracked ALS progression over time, highlighting their potential as endpoints for future clinical trials. A larger, formally powered study is required to further support activity endpoints as novel disease progression markers.

combined_corpus.title: Analyzing progression of motor and speech impairment in ALS.
combined_corpus.journal: Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society. IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society. Annual International Conference
combined_corpus.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a degenerative disease which causes death of neurons controlling voluntary muscles. It is currently assessed with subjective clinical measurements, but it would benefit from alternative surrogate biomarkers that can better estimate disease progression. This work analyzes speech and fine motor coordination of subjects recruited by the Answer ALS foundation using data from a mobile app. In addition, clinical variables such as speech, writing and total ALSFRS-R scores are also acquired along with forced and slow vital capacity. Cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses were performed using speech and fine motor features. Results show that both types of features are useful to infer clinical variables especially for males (R2=0.79 for ALSFRS-R total score), but their initial values are not helpful to predict speech and motor decline. However, we found that longitudinal progression for bulbar and spinal ALS onset are different and they can be identified with high accuracy by the extracted features.

combined_corpus.title: Automated speech analytics in ALS: higher sensitivity of digital articulatory precision over the ALSFRS-R.
combined_corpus.abstract: Objective: Although studies have shown that digital measures of speech detected ALS speech impairment and correlated with the ALSFRS-R speech item, no study has yet compared their performance in detecting speech changes. In this study, we compared the performances of the ALSFRS-R speech item and an algorithmic speech measure in detecting clinically important changes in speech. Importantly, the study was part of a FDA submission which received the breakthrough device designation for monitoring ALS; we provide this paper as a roadmap for validating other speech measures for monitoring disease progression. Methods: We obtained ALSFRS-R speech subscores and speech samples from participants with ALS. We computed the minimum detectable change (MDC) of both measures; using clinician-reported listener effort and a perceptual ratings of severity, we calculated the minimal clinically important difference (MCID) of each measure with respect to both sets of clinical ratings. Results: For articulatory precision, the MDC (.85) was lower than both MCID measures (2.74 and 2.28), and for the ALSFRS-R speech item, MDC (.86) was greater than both MCID measures (.82 and .72), indicating that while the articulatory precision measure detected minimal clinically important differences in speech, the ALSFRS-R speech item did not. Conclusion: The results demonstrate that the digital measure of articulatory precision effectively detects clinically important differences in speech ratings, outperforming the ALSFRS-R speech item. Taken together, the results herein suggest that this speech outcome is a clinically meaningful measure of speech change.

combined_devices.limitations: Publication count limited to 2 peer-reviewed papers; samples 28–32 patients; all clinical evidence closely tied to sponsoring company; no MID/MICD established; no reported test-retest reliability or SEM/MDC; regulatory clearance status unknown; algorithm version pinning not documented. Tier 1 assigned under nascent-COI criteria (best-available candidate warranting further development, not trial-ready for primary/secondary endpoint).

Feasibility study only (N=10); no clinical validation or correlation with disease measures; no longitudinal data; no trial endpoint use; specific extracted oculometric parameters not reported; no regulatory pathway; smartphone-based acquisition not yet validated against clinical-grade systems for quantitative biomarker extraction. Milestone to Tier 2: clinical validation study with ALSFRS-R correlation in ≥15 ALS patients.

combined_devices.manufacturer: Multiple (not specified in most records; generic device class)
combined_devices.limitations: Examiner-induced ceiling effect when patient strength exceeds examiner strength (PMID:33355879 documents range limited to 414N); requires trained examiner and clinic visit; higher within- and among-person variability than fixed dynamometry (PMID:28120413); less sensitive to change than ATLIS.

Single cross-sectional study only (N=54); no longitudinal data; no trial endpoint use; single-site clinic-based; correlation with split-hand index is scientifically interesting but does not establish endpoint utility; no MID, SEM, or test-retest reliability reported; described as needing 'further study' by authors themselves.

combined_gaps.action: Anchor-based MID study linking oculometric change to patient-meaningful clinical change (e.g., ALSFRS-R 1-point bulbar decline) required before primary/secondary endpoint use.

combined_gaps.description: No test-retest reliability (ICC), standard error of measurement (SEM), or minimal detectable change (MDC) reported for NeuraLight oculometric measures.
combined_gaps.action: Repeat-assessment study (same patients, short interval with expected clinical stability) needed to establish measurement precision.

combined_gaps.description: No FDA or EMA guidance document names oculomotor function as a recognized or encouraged endpoint in ALS. No Biomarker Development Data (BDD) submission or qualification pathway identified for oculometrics.
combined_gaps.action: Search FDA DDT/BQP databases and EMA qualification opinions for oculomotor/eye-tracking biomarker submissions; if absent, a Letter of Intent or pre-submission meeting would be the first regulatory step.

combined_gaps.description: NeuraLight platform regulatory clearance status (FDA 510(k), De Novo, CE mark) not documented in available corpus.
combined_gaps.action: Query NeuraLight directly and search FDA accessdata.fda.gov for any clearance under product code or company name.

combined_gaps.description: All published NeuraLight clinical evidence originates from what appears to be a single collaborating research group; no independent replication by external investigators.
combined_gaps.action: Identify whether NCT05407428 (N=45, completed) results have been published and whether any independent academic groups have used the platform; request vendor list of collaborating sites.

combined_gaps.description: Largest published patient sample is N=32 (PMID:37026395). No study exceeds N=50 patients in the available corpus, limiting statistical power for subgroup analyses and generalizability.
combined_gaps.action: Determine whether the completed N=45 study (NCT05407428) has published results; if so, retrieve and score.

combined_gaps.description: Algorithm version pinning and measure-fidelity documentation for NeuraLight platform not available in corpus. Cannot confirm whether oculometric extraction algorithms are locked across studies or evolved between PMID:37026395 and PMID:38975625.
combined_gaps.action: Request algorithm version history, change log, and any equivalence data between versions from NeuraLight.

combined_gaps.description: Concurrent validity of NeuraLight vs. clinical-grade VOG or research eye-tracker (e.g., EyeLink) not published in available corpus; NCT05407428 lists this as an outcome but results not yet available.
combined_gaps.action: Retrieve NCT05407428 results when published; alternatively request unpublished comparison data from NeuraLight.

combined_gaps.description: Power calculation in PMID:38975625 (150 per arm for 40% bulbar reduction) assumes effect size from a single 28-patient cohort. Sensitivity analysis with varying assumptions not provided.
combined_gaps.action: Request full statistical analysis plan and sensitivity analyses from study authors or NeuraLight.

combined_gaps.description: Specific HHD device brands/models (e.g., MicroFET, Biodex) are not named in the corpus — the device class is validated but manufacturer-level assessment is impossible without brand identification.
combined_gaps.action: Identify specific HHD brands used in Phase 3 ALS trials (NCT03127267, NCT04248465) via full-text methods sections or vendor inquiries.

combined_gaps.description: ATLIS has not been used as a pre-specified secondary endpoint in a Phase 3 RCT despite superior sensitivity to change; its trial endpoint fit remains at Phase 2 level.
combined_gaps.action: Search for ongoing Phase 3 trials specifying ATLIS; if none, this represents a validation-to-deployment gap requiring sponsor engagement.

combined_gaps.description: No MID/MICD (minimal important difference / minimal important clinical difference) is established for any dynamometry-based muscle strength measure in ALS within this corpus.
combined_gaps.action: Conduct anchor-based MID analysis using ALSFRS-R change as anchor, or identify published MID studies outside this corpus. Without MID, sample size calculations for primary/secondary endpoints cannot be finalized.

combined_gaps.description: Portable Fixed Dynamometer (PFD) has strong home-use reliability/sensitivity data but zero interventional trial use. The device-to-trial translation has not occurred.
combined_gaps.action: Determine if PFD is being incorporated into any upcoming ALS trial protocol; if not, a pilot substudy embedding PFD alongside HHD in a planned trial would generate concurrent validity and trial-readiness data.

combined_gaps.description: PFD evidence comes from what appears to be a single research group — no independent replication of reliability or longitudinal sensitivity findings.
combined_gaps.action: Identify whether independent groups have published on PFD or similar portable fixed dynamometry in ALS/MND; if not, independent replication is a prerequisite for Tier 1 advancement.

combined_gaps.description: No FDA or EMA guidance document specifically endorsing dynamometry-based muscle strength as a primary or secondary endpoint in ALS was identified in this corpus. Trial use is established by precedent, not by explicit agency endorsement.
combined_gaps.action: Search FDA guidance for ALS drug development and EMA qualification opinions for dynamometry endpoints; check if any Biomarker Qualification Program submission covers muscle strength in ALS.

combined_gaps.description: Head-to-head comparison between ATLIS and PFD has not been conducted. Both claim superiority over HHD via different mechanisms (fixed frame eliminating examiner ceiling vs. portability enabling home use), but relative performance is unknown.
combined_gaps.action: Design or identify a protocol that deploys both ATLIS (clinic) and PFD (home) in the same ALS cohort to compare sensitivity to change and feasibility.

combined_gaps.description: Activity tracker brand, algorithm, and specific validation for ALS muscle function are absent from this corpus — the device is mentioned but never characterized.
combined_gaps.action: Identify specific accelerometer/activity-tracker brands used in PMID:32515889 and PMID:30486680 via full text; assess whether any have ALS-specific validation for motor decline detection.

combined_gaps.description: REFINE-ALS (PMID:34476128) protocol describes collection of HHD and grip strength in 200+ edaravone-treated patients with longitudinal biomarker correlation — results not yet available in this corpus but would substantially strengthen the evidence base.
combined_gaps.action: Check whether REFINE-ALS results have been published; if available, the HHD longitudinal data with biomarker correlations would inform treatment-sensitivity assessment.

combined_gaps.description: No Minimal Important Difference (MID) or Minimal Clinically Important Difference (MCID) established for Reachable Workspace RSA or any digital ROM measure in ALS
combined_gaps.action: Anchor-based MID study needed using ALSFRS-R UE subscale as anchor in ≥30 ALS patients with longitudinal RSA data; required before any primary/secondary endpoint recommendation

combined_gaps.description: No RCT has used RSA or any digital ROM measure as a pre-specified primary or secondary endpoint in ALS
combined_gaps.action: Identify ongoing or planned ALS interventional trials incorporating RSA as exploratory endpoint to generate precedent; alternatively design a dedicated endpoint qualification study

combined_gaps.description: No currently-marketed commercial device is specifically validated and available for ALS ROM endpoint measurement; Microsoft Kinect hardware lifecycle uncertain with review indicating field transition to newer mobile sensing technologies
combined_gaps.action: Identify successor 3D depth sensors (e.g., Azure Kinect, Intel RealSense, LiDAR) and determine whether RWS RSA validation transfers; may require bridging validation study comparing Kinect vs. successor in ≥10 ALS patients

combined_gaps.description: All ALS ROM validation studies limited to N≤13 patients per study; no multi-site validation exists for any digital ROM measure in ALS
combined_gaps.action: Multi-site study with N≥50 ALS patients across disease severity spectrum needed to establish measurement properties (reliability, SEM, floor/ceiling effects) at adequate power

combined_gaps.description: No home/remote validation of RWS measurement in ALS — all studies clinic-based with controlled conditions and supervised setup
combined_gaps.action: Feasibility study of unsupervised home-based RWS acquisition using consumer-grade depth sensor or IMU-based alternative; assess data quality, completion rate, and agreement with clinic measurement

combined_gaps.description: No test-retest reliability (ICC), Standard Error of Measurement (SEM), or within-session repeatability reported for RSA in ALS patients
combined_gaps.action: Test-retest study with repeated assessments within 1-2 weeks in stable ALS patients to establish ICC and SEM; required for sample size calculation

combined_gaps.description: No FDA or EMA guidance specifically names digital ROM or RSA as an acceptable endpoint in ALS; RWS COA tool status is field-recognized but not formally qualified
combined_gaps.action: Search FDA Drug Development Tool qualification submissions and EMA qualification opinions for RWS/RSA; if absent, consider Clinical Outcome Assessment qualification pathway

combined_gaps.description: Head-neck IMU approach (PMID:28068376) lacks named commercial product, correlation with clinical scales, and longitudinal validation — cannot assess trial-readiness
combined_gaps.action: Identify commercial IMU platforms (e.g., APDM/Clario, Xsens) that could implement head-neck kinematics protocol; request or conduct correlation study with ALSFRS-R in ≥20 ALS patients with longitudinal follow-up

combined_gaps.description: Specific commercial spirometer devices used in major ALS remote monitoring studies are not named in the corpus. The HEALEY Platform Trial, COURAGE-ALS, Radcliff Study, and Hershey rPFT studies all use home spirometers but do not specify brand/model in available abstracts.
combined_gaps.action: Full-text retrieval of key studies (PMID:42366580, PMID:40630909, PMID:38522245) to identify specific devices; vendor outreach to manufacturers of ALS-validated spirometers (likely candidates: MIR Spirobank Smart, ndd EasyOne Air, Vitalograph, Vyaire)

combined_gaps.description: No MID/MICD (Minimal Important Difference / Minimal Important Clinical Difference) published specifically for home-measured vital capacity in ALS. This is essential for primary endpoint sample size calculations.
combined_gaps.action: Literature search for FVC/SVC MID in ALS (may exist for clinic-measured VC but applicability to home VC with 28% faster progression rate is uncertain); anchor-based and distribution-based MID estimation from existing longitudinal datasets (HEALEY, Radcliff)

combined_gaps.description: At-home VC progresses 28% faster than in-clinic VC with proportionately less variance (at-home -1.882 vs in-clinic -1.476 %PN/month). This systematic difference is unexplained and complicates equivalence claims between settings.
combined_gaps.action: Determine whether the faster at-home rate reflects true biology (effort differences, coaching effects, regression to mean at clinic) vs. measurement artifact; resolve before at-home VC can substitute for in-clinic VC as a primary endpoint

combined_gaps.description: No FDA or EMA qualification opinion or guidance document specifically endorsing home-measured FVC/SVC as a primary or secondary endpoint in ALS trials. Vital capacity is accepted as an endpoint from clinic measurement, but the regulatory position on remote/home measurement equivalence is not established in this corpus.
combined_gaps.action: Search FDA DDT/COA qualification database and EMA qualification advice for respiratory endpoints in ALS; check whether HEALEY Platform Trial's FDA IND included home VC as qualified endpoint; contact regulatory intelligence

combined_gaps.description: Remote FVC completion rates significantly lower than in-clinic (82% vs 96%, p<0.001 in COURAGE-ALS). 65% of site personnel reported technological challenges. Missing data patterns for home-measured respiratory function may introduce bias in trial analyses.
combined_gaps.action: Evaluate missingness patterns from HEALEY and COURAGE-ALS for informative vs. random missing data; develop mitigation strategies (mixed models, sensitivity analyses); determine minimum acceptable completion rate threshold

combined_gaps.description: No SEM (Standard Error of Measurement) or MDC (Minimal Detectable Change) reported for any home spirometry platform in ALS within this corpus. These are prerequisite for distinguishing measurement noise from true progression.
combined_gaps.action: Calculate within-session and between-session variability from published or available repeated-measures datasets; the Radcliff Study (PMID:40630909) with its automated QC may have these data internally

combined_gaps.description: No regulatory clearance information (510(k), CE mark, or equivalent) cited for any specific home spirometry device used in ALS studies. Cannot confirm whether devices used have appropriate intended-use claims for clinical trial endpoint measurement vs. general wellness.
combined_gaps.action: Identify specific devices from full-text papers and cross-reference FDA 510(k) database (accessdata.fda.gov) and EU EUDAMED; determine whether intended use covers unsupervised home measurement by patients with neuromuscular disease

combined_gaps.description: Sound-based CPF smartphone app (PMID:37860934) has no longitudinal validation in ALS. ICC of 0.774 is below the 0.80 threshold typically required for measurement substitution. No trial deployment.
combined_gaps.action: Monitor for longitudinal follow-up study; not actionable for near-term endpoint use without repeated-measures and remote deployment validation

combined_gaps.description: KOKA peak flow meter validated in a single Chinese cohort only. No Western population validation, no home-use deployment data, no trial endpoint use for PEF in ALS.
combined_gaps.action: Determine device availability outside China; search for independent replication of PEF as ALS progression marker; assess whether PEF adds value beyond FVC/SVC given high correlation (r=0.797)

combined_gaps.description: Multiple ongoing/recently completed trials (NCT05106569, n=100 telespirometry; NCT07071935 EVENT ALS) may have unpublished data on home respiratory monitoring performance that could resolve gaps in SEM/MDC and device identification.
combined_gaps.action: Check clinicaltrials.gov results postings for NCT05106569 (status: UNKNOWN); monitor NCT07071935 for interim data; contact Milton S. Hershey group for NCT03214224/NCT04490148 results

combined_gaps.description: The speech analytics platform with FDA Breakthrough Device Designation is not explicitly named (manufacturer and product name) in any available abstract. Platform identity across articulatory precision, timing alignment, and multimodal papers is inferred from shared trial use (VRG50635) but not confirmed as a single commercial product.
combined_gaps.action: Identify manufacturer and product name from FDA Breakthrough Device database (accessdata.fda.gov De Novo or Breakthrough listings); confirm whether articulatory precision and multimodal/timing measures are from the same or different commercial platforms; obtain vendor materials and regulatory submission documents.

combined_gaps.description: No device in this landscape has achieved 510(k), De Novo, or PMA clearance for speech endpoint measurement in ALS. The FDA Breakthrough Device Designation is a pathway designation only, not a marketing authorization. Regulatory clearance status of ki:SB-M is unknown.
combined_gaps.action: Search FDA accessdata.fda.gov for speech/acoustic analysis devices with neurological indications; query ki elements for CE marking or FDA submission status; determine whether Breakthrough designation has progressed to submission.

combined_gaps.description: No Phase 2 or Phase 3 trial in the corpus uses a digital speech measure as a pre-specified primary endpoint. VRG50635 is Phase 1b and speech measures were secondary/exploratory. The evidence supports secondary endpoint use but primary endpoint readiness is undemonstrated.
combined_gaps.action: Search ClinicalTrials.gov for ongoing/planned ALS trials with digital speech as primary or key secondary endpoint; query FDA Division of Neurology Products for guidance on speech endpoint qualification in ALS; assess whether existing responsiveness data (PMID:39126786) supports powering a Phase 2 trial.

combined_gaps.description: MDC/MCID is established only for articulatory precision (PMID:38932502). No other platform (ki:SB-M, Everything ALS listener effort, ALS TDI ML score, timing alignment) reports MDC or MCID. Without MCID, sample size calculations for trials using these measures are not anchored.
combined_gaps.action: For ki:SB-M: request from vendor or compute from published longitudinal data (PMID:40851280). For listener effort: compute from ICC and longitudinal variance in Everything ALS dataset. For timing alignment: derive from responsiveness data in PMID:39126786.

combined_gaps.description: Head-to-head comparison between Tier 1/2 platforms is absent. No study compares articulatory precision vs. ki:SB-M intelligibility vs. listener effort vs. timing alignment in the same ALS cohort. Platform selection for a trial cannot be evidence-based without comparative data.
combined_gaps.action: Design a concordance study or identify ongoing multi-platform studies (e.g., NCT06885918 with N=2000 may collect multiple speech measures); query vendors about willingness to participate in comparative validation.

combined_gaps.description: Floor/ceiling effects in severe bulbar ALS are not systematically characterized for any platform. Patients who cannot produce intelligible speech or complete passage reading tasks cannot be assessed — exclusion rate and measurement floor are unreported.
combined_gaps.action: Request from vendors data on task completion rates by ALSFRS-R bulbar subscore stratum; determine minimum speech production threshold for each platform; assess whether alternative tasks (sustained phonation, tongue movement) extend measurable range.

combined_gaps.description: Linguistic and cultural validation beyond English is limited. ki:SB-M has multi-language data (Czech, Colombian Spanish, German per PMID:39108340); the articulatory precision platform's language coverage is unclear. NCT07341334 (Spanish) is not yet recruiting.
combined_gaps.action: Confirm language availability from each vendor; if trial is multinational, assess whether cross-language validation supports pooled analysis or requires language-specific norms.

combined_gaps.description: NCT04889898 (Beiwe app, N=200, completed) and NCT06885918 (ALS TDI, N=2000, recruiting) represent substantial datasets whose results are not yet published in the available corpus. These may substantially alter the landscape upon publication.
combined_gaps.action: Monitor for publication of NCT04889898 results; request interim data from ALS TDI on NCT06885918 speech scoring validation; assess whether either dataset includes head-to-head platform comparisons.

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Oculomotor function in ALS encompasses eye-movement parameters—saccadic intrusions (square wave jerks), saccadic latency, gain, and velocity, smooth pursuit fixation, and anti-saccade error rate—extracted via software-based eye-tracking platforms as objective biomarkers of disease state and progression, with particular relevance to bulbar function.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: Oculometric measures correlate with ALSFRS-R (R=0.52 corrective saccadic latency; R=-0.43 to -0.47 SWJ vs. bulbar subscale) and show longitudinal sensitivity to change in ALS patients (PMID:38975625, PMID:37026395).
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: No FDA/EMA guidance names oculomotor function as a recognized ALS endpoint; use to date is exploratory within Phase IIb drug trials only (PMID:37026395, NCT05407428).
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Software-based oculometric platforms have been deployed in completed multi-patient clinical trials (N=28–45) and a smartphone self-recording solution has demonstrated feasibility (NCT05407428, PMID:39015513).

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Square wave jerk (SWJ) frequency, amplitude, and duration during fixation; corrective saccadic latency; pro-saccade gain and peak velocity
measurement_spec.algorithm: Proprietary software-based extraction (NeuraLight platform); specific algorithm versions not published in available corpus

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Progressive loss of voluntary muscle strength due to upper and lower motor neuron degeneration in ALS, quantified via isometric dynamometry (handheld, fixed, or portable), grip strength, and composite muscle strength megascores. Decline in muscle strength is a cardinal disease feature that precedes functional loss on rating scales and correlates with disease progression across ALS phenotypes.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: Muscle weakness is the defining clinical feature of ALS; multiple Phase 3 RCTs use dynamometry-derived strength as a pre-specified secondary endpoint (NCT03127267, NCT04248465, NCT04944784), and phenotype-specific strength trajectories are documented (PMID:42267597).
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: Handheld dynamometry and ATLIS are pre-specified secondary or exploratory endpoints in completed and ongoing Phase 2/3 ALS trials (NCT03127514, NCT04248465, NCT04944784); FDA-approved edaravone's REFINE-ALS study includes HHD (PMID:34476128).
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Multiple device classes with published reliability data exist: HHD (widely deployed), ATLIS with ICC and SRM data (PMID:28120413), PFD with ICC 0.97 and home-use feasibility (PMID:37427714, PMID:33355879), and grip dynamometers validated for daily home use (PMID:32515889).

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Isometric muscle strength megascore (aggregate of multiple muscle groups, expressed as %predicted or raw Newtons/pounds)
measurement_spec.epoch: Monthly (clinic-based) or biweekly-to-daily (home-based); PMID:32515889 demonstrates daily feasibility
measurement_spec.algorithm: Maximum voluntary isometric contraction (MVIC); fixed dynamometry preferred to reduce examiner variability per PMID:28120413 and PMID:33355879
measurement_spec.valid_day_rule: Not standardized in this corpus; PMID:37427714 uses consecutive unsupervised measurements for test-retest; PMID:32515889 uses minimum 7 days data for inclusion

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Range of motion (ROM) in ALS encompasses progressive limitation of voluntary movement amplitude due to motor neuron degeneration. The primary digitally-validated construct is Reachable Workspace Relative Surface Area (RSA), quantifying 3D upper extremity mobility normalized by arm length. Secondary constructs include head-neck kinematics (angular velocity, smoothness, movement coupling) capturing cervical ROM impairment including head drop syndrome.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: RSA demonstrates significant longitudinal decline in ALS (upper lateral quadrant ~19%, p<0.01 over 12 months) and correlates with ALSFRS-R UE subscale (PMID:27813421, PMID:25965847); head-neck kinematics show significant ALS-vs-control group differences in velocity and smoothness (PMID:28068376); robotic brace measures correlate with ALSFRS-R and FVC (PMID:31392848)
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: RWS recognized as a clinical outcome assessment (COA) tool across neuromuscular diseases after more than a decade of clinical study use (PMID:41960733), but no specific FDA/EMA qualification pathway or endpoint acceptance documented for ALS ROM in this corpus; no device cleared specifically for ALS ROM endpoint measurement
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: 3D depth sensors and IMUs are mature hardware classes with demonstrated ALS feasibility (PMID:25965847, PMID:28068376), but no single commercial product is currently validated and marketed specifically for ALS ROM clinical endpoint use; narrative review indicates transition to newer mobile sensing technologies is underway (PMID:41960733)

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Reachable Workspace Relative Surface Area (RSA), specifically upper lateral quadrant RSA (most sensitive to longitudinal decline)
measurement_spec.epoch: Single clinic-based assessment session (bilateral 3D workspace sweep, self-selected speed)
measurement_spec.algorithm: 3D surface area reconstruction from depth-sensor skeletal tracking, normalized by individual arm length to yield RSA; quadrant decomposition (upper/lower × lateral/medial)

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Respiratory function in ALS reflects progressive ventilatory capacity decline due to motor neuron degeneration affecting diaphragm, intercostal, and accessory respiratory muscles. Key measures include vital capacity (FVC/SVC, % predicted), peak expiratory flow (PEF), maximum inspiratory/expiratory pressures (MIP/MEP), sniff nasal inspiratory pressure (SNIP), and cough peak flow (CPF). FVC/SVC < 50% predicted is the standard threshold for NIV initiation and a recognized marker of respiratory failure progression.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: Respiratory failure is the primary cause of death in ALS; FVC/SVC decline is a hallmark disease feature used as an inclusion criterion and endpoint in major Phase 2/3 trials including VITALITY-ALS (PMID:33792451), Triumeq Phase 3 (NCT05193994), and HEALEY Platform Trial (PMID:42366580).
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: Vital capacity (FVC/SVC) is used as a secondary/primary endpoint in completed Phase 2/3 ALS trials (NCT05193994, PMID:33792451, PMID:27581221); at-home VC has been deployed at scale in the HEALEY ALS Platform Trial (PMID:42366580) and COURAGE-ALS Phase 3 (PMID:40576049).
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Home spirometry devices are commercially available, have demonstrated concordance with clinic spirometry (Lin's rc = 0.82, PMID:42366580), and automated quality control protocols have been validated (PMID:40630909).

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Vital Capacity (SVC or FVC), expressed as % predicted normal
measurement_spec.epoch: Weekly (optimal for power gain per PMID:32515889); monthly acceptable with reduced power; quarterly insufficient for remote monitoring benefit
measurement_spec.algorithm: Standard spirometry per ATS/ERS acceptability and repeatability criteria; automated quality control protocol demonstrated feasible (PMID:40630909)
measurement_spec.valid_day_rule: Predefined automatic quality control per ATS/ERS standards; coached via live videoconference with central review (PMID:42366580); minimum 3 acceptable efforts per session

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Bulbar speech (dysarthria) in ALS refers to progressive motor speech impairment caused by upper and lower motor neuron degeneration affecting articulatory, phonatory, resonatory, and respiratory subsystems. Up to 25% of people with ALS exhibit bulbar dysfunction at diagnosis, and over 80% develop speech difficulties during the disease course. Key constructs include articulatory precision, speech intelligibility, speaking rate, pause characteristics, and listener effort.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: Bulbar dysfunction is a cardinal feature of ALS affecting communication, quality of life, and linked to shorter survival; digital speech measures correlate strongly with bulbar-specific clinical scores and detect decline before ALSFRS-R speech item (PMID:38932502, PMID:33083567, PMID:38062079, PMID:39126786)
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: FDA Breakthrough Device Designation granted for a digital articulatory precision measure for monitoring ALS speech progression (PMID:38932502, PMID:38838248)
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Multiple validated platforms demonstrate remote smartphone/app-based speech collection with automated algorithmic scoring, longitudinal sensitivity, and use in Phase 1b clinical trial (PMID:41981045, PMID:33083567, PMID:39126786, PMID:42159474)

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Articulatory precision (phoneme-level acoustic crispness score); secondary: speaking rate, timing alignment, speech intelligibility
measurement_spec.epoch: Bi-weekly speech assessment (passage reading, sustained phonation, syllable repetition tasks); daily/weekly collection feasible
measurement_spec.algorithm: Phoneme-level acoustic signal analysis computing articulatory precision; ASR-based intelligibility scoring; timing alignment relative to canonical elicitation
measurement_spec.valid_day_rule: Completion of standardized passage reading task (e.g., 99-word passage or Bamboo Passage); minimum recording quality threshold for automated processing
